critic_raw:
This is a generally well-designed experiment. The temporal split is correct, leakage controls are thoughtfully enumerated, and the evaluation suite is appropriate for the domain. I will work through the real issues I see, organized by root cause.

---

**Group 1: Label definition creates a structural leakage risk via the 24-hour contact-intensity score**

The label is defined as escalation within 2 hours of ticket opening. The contact-intensity score is described as 'a rolling 24-hour account contact-intensity score summarizing support touches and routing events.' If this score is computed over the 24 hours preceding the ticket, it captures the same clustered-interaction pattern that drives escalation — but if the 24-hour window is centered on or extends past ticket creation time, it includes post-creation events that are causally downstream of or concurrent with the urgency state. The methodology says future information is excluded, but this is asserted rather than demonstrated for this specific feature.

The claim being made: the contact-intensity score reflects only information available at the moment of ticket creation.

Why it might be wrong: 'Rolling 24-hour' is ambiguous about window alignment. If the score is computed as a trailing window ending at ticket-open time, it is clean. If the window is centered on ticket time or updated in real time during the session, it will absorb the opening interaction itself, sister escalation events within the same burst, or other post-open signals — all of which are correlated with the label by construction.

Evidence: Compute the contact-intensity score for tickets with artificially set timestamps (t=0) and compare it to the score computed at t-1 minute. If they differ substantially, the window is contaminated. A simpler check: regress the feature alone against the label — if AUC is implausibly high (>0.8) for a single rolling count feature, leakage is likely.

---

**Group 2: The inter-rater filtering protocol conflates label noise with systematic disagreement, and may bias the training distribution**

The methodology excludes tickets with inter-rater agreement below 80% on a 500-ticket sample. This filtering is applied globally — it is unclear whether disagreement-prone tickets are identified and removed from all three splits or only used to justify confidence in overall label quality.

If filtering removes tickets from training (but not from test), the training distribution is cleaner than the deployment distribution — the model never learns to handle the ambiguous cases agents actually escalate inconsistently.

More fundamentally, the 80% threshold on 500 sampled tickets produces a ticket-type-level exclusion signal, not a case-level exclusion. Low-agreement tickets are likely concentrated in specific case types (borderline complaints, tier-2 routing gray areas). Removing them selectively depresses coverage on exactly the hard cases where urgency classification is most valuable.

Claim: inter-rater filtering reduces noise without biasing the evaluation distribution.

Why it might be wrong: Disagreement is not random — it is concentrated in structurally ambiguous ticket types. Removing them from training makes the problem artificially easier, and if those ticket types appear in deployment (they will), the model has never been trained to handle them.

Evidence: Characterize the excluded tickets by ticket type, channel, and customer tier. If excluded tickets cluster in specific strata (e.g., chat-channel tier-2 customers with partial disruption flags), the model has a structural coverage gap. Compare model performance on held-out excluded tickets versus included tickets.

---

**Group 3: The baseline is not a true history-ablation — it confounds model architecture with feature set**

The proposed model is a shared DistilBERT encoder with joint encoding of current + historical text + structured features, fed into a 2-layer MLP. The baseline is logistic regression on the current [CLS] token only. This comparison tests two things simultaneously: (a) the value of historical features, and (b) the value of a nonlinear MLP head versus logistic regression. If the joint model outperforms the baseline, the improvement could be due entirely to the MLP's capacity on the current message alone, not the historical context.

Claim: any improvement over the baseline is attributable to joint encoding of history.

Why it might be wrong: A DistilBERT + MLP on current message alone would likely outperform logistic regression on the same embedding. The baseline controls for the embedding pipeline but not for classifier capacity.

Evidence: Add a third condition — DistilBERT + 2-layer MLP on current message only (no history, no structured features). If this condition closes most of the gap between baseline and proposed model, the history features are not doing meaningful work. This ablation is the minimum required to isolate the hypothesis.

---

**Group 4: Threshold selection on validation F1 (macro) is potentially unstable under class imbalance**

The decision threshold is selected by optimizing macro F1 on the validation set, then frozen for test. Macro F1 treats both classes equally, but the minority class (urgent) is likely 10–20% of tickets (a common rate for escalated airline support). At this imbalance level, macro F1 is sensitive to small absolute changes in the minority class recall, which means the optimal threshold can shift substantially across temporal windows — precisely the kind of drift documented in the setup.

If the urgency rate in July–December 2024 differs from January–June 2024 (plausible: the test window includes summer travel disruption peaks), the frozen threshold will be systematically miscalibrated.

Claim: the threshold selected on validation generalizes to the test period.

Why it might be wrong: Class prevalence shifts between validation and test windows due to seasonal effects or operational policy changes. The frozen threshold will be either too aggressive or too conservative, making the test F1 an artifact of temporal prevalence drift rather than a measure of classifier quality.

Evidence: Report the urgency prevalence rate separately for each month of training, validation, and test windows. If it varies by more than 5 percentage points, report threshold-free metrics (AUC-PR) as primary and treat frozen-threshold F1 as secondary. The methodology does include AUC-PR as secondary — this should be elevated to co-primary.

---

**Summary of genuine issues:**
1. Contact-intensity score window alignment is unverified and may carry structural leakage.
2. Inter-rater filtering may selectively remove the hard cases the model most needs to handle.
3. The baseline conflates feature ablation with model capacity — a current-message MLP ablation is missing.
4. Frozen threshold under temporal prevalence shift may make test F1 misleading.

Issues 1 and 3 are the most fundamental. Issue 1 could invalidate the feature entirely; issue 3 means the core comparative claim cannot be made from the current experimental design.
all_issues_raised: The 24-hour contact-intensity score has an unverified window alignment that may include post-ticket-creation events causally downstream of the label, creating structural feature leakage.
Inter-rater filtering removes structurally ambiguous tickets that are likely concentrated in specific ticket types, biasing training away from the hard cases most relevant to deployment.

The baseline confounds feature ablation with model capacity by comparing DistilBERT+MLP (with history) to logistic regression (without history), omitting the necessary DistilBERT+MLP current-message-only ablation.
Freezing the decision threshold at validation macro-F1 optimum may be miscalibrated for the test window if urgency prevalence shifts seasonally between the two periods.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 24-hour contact-intensity score has an unverified window alignment that may include post-ticket-creation events causally downstream of the label, creating structural feature leakage.
Inter-rater filtering removes structurally ambiguous tickets that are likely concentrated in specific ticket types, biasing training away from the hard cases most relevant to deployment.

The baseline confounds feature ablation with model capacity by comparing DistilBERT+MLP (with history) to logistic regression (without history), omitting the necessary DistilBERT+MLP current-message-only ablation.
Freezing the decision threshold at validation macro-F1 optimum may be miscalibrated for the test window if urgency prevalence shifts seasonally between the two periods.
